questions.q: A moduli problem for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ is encoded by a functor $\mathcal{M}\colon\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$. What does $\mathcal{M}(S)$ contain?
choices: the set of closed points of $S$
isomorphism classes of families $E\to S$ whose geometric fibres are elliptic curves
the set of line bundles on $S$
the category of all schemes over $S$
questions.explain: The moduli functor sends a base $S$ to iso classes of families $E\to S$ of the objects of interest — here, flat proper families of elliptic curves.

questions.q: Over $\mathbb{C}$, the $j$-invariant induces a bijection between iso classes of elliptic curves and $\mathbb{A}^1(\mathbb{C})=\mathbb{C}$. What is the (complex) dimension of this parameter space?
For the curve $y^2=x^3+1$ (so $a=0$, $b=1$), compute the discriminant $\Delta=-16(4a^3+27b^2)$ as $a+bi$.
questions.explain: $\Delta=-16(0+27)=-16\cdot 27=-432$, which is nonzero, confirming the curve is smooth (hence an elliptic curve).

questions.q: Which statement characterises a fine moduli space $M$ for $\mathcal{M}$?
choices: $\mathcal{M}(\mathrm{Spec}\,\bar k)\to M(\bar k)$ is a bijection for every algebraically closed $k$
$\mathcal{M}$ is naturally isomorphic to $h_M=\operatorname{Hom}(-,M)$, so there exists a universal family $\mathcal{E}\to M$
$M$ is the quotient of a scheme by a finite group
questions.explain: Fine moduli is exactly representability: $\mathcal{M}\cong h_M$. The identity $\mathrm{id}_M\in h_M(M)$ corresponds to the universal family $\mathcal{E}\to M$.

questions.q: The $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ is a moduli space for elliptic curves. Which type?
choices: it is not a moduli space in any sense
it is a compact moduli space
questions.explain: The $j$-line is the coarse moduli space of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$: geometric points match iso classes bijectively, but no universal family exists because of automorphisms.

questions.q: The obstruction to fine moduli for $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is the presence of nontrivial automorphisms. Every elliptic curve has at least the $[-1]$ involution. What is the order of the automorphism group of a generic elliptic curve (with $j\ne 0,1728$)?
questions.explain: For generic $j$, the only nontrivial automorphism is $[-1]:(x,y)\mapsto(x,-y)$, so $|\mathrm{Aut}(E)|=2$.

questions.q: Why is a level-$N$ structure required to satisfy $N\ge 3$ to rigidify $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ into a fine moduli space?
choices: For $N=2$, $[-1]$ acts as the identity on $E[2]$ (since $-P=P$ for $2$-torsion), so it still fixes every level-$2$ basis
For $N=2$, the curve $Y(2)$ does not exist
questions.explain: On $E[2]$, multiplication by $-1$ is the identity because $2P=0$ implies $-P=P$. So $[-1]$ fixes any basis of $E[2]$, i.e. level-$2$ is not rigid enough. For $N\ge 3$, $[-1]\ne 1$ on $E[N]$.

questions.q: For smooth projective curves of genus $g\ge 2$, $\dim M_g=3g-3$. Compute $\dim M_4$.
questions.explain: $3(4)-3=9$. This is the deformation-theoretic count $\dim H^1(C,T_C)$.

questions.q: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ adds stable curves. Using $\dim M_{g,n}=3g-3+n$ for $g\ge 2$, compute $\dim M_{2,3}$.
